evidence_quotes.quote: video game preservation and digital ownership rights.
shut down access to games consumers have purchased.

paragraphs.text: I am writing to support stronger consumer protections in the Digital Fairness Act regarding video game preservation and digital ownership rights. As part of the Stop Killing Games initiative, I urge action against companies that shut down access to games consumers have purchased. Modern games increasingly depend on servers or online checks even when core gameplay doesn't require connectivity. This allows companies to remotely disable products consumers legally own, undermining basic ownership rights and threatening digital media preservation. Publishers routinely shut down functional games without technical justification, making full-price purchases inaccessible within months or years. They refuse to provide offline patches and legally prevent community restoration efforts, effectively deleting digital property consumers bought in good faith. This practice harms consumers and culture by devaluing digital ownership, erasing artistic works, preventing preservation efforts, and fostering market distrust. No other medium faces such deliberate destruction. I urge lawmakers to include these protections in the Digital Fairness Act: Mandatory offline patches when shutting down servers, especially for single-player games Legal protection for preservation groups to restore abandoned games Advance notice requirements and transparency about post-shutdown functionality Consumer rights to offline versions or refunds when games are discontinued DRM restrictions that prevent tools from becoming instruments of digital destruction Without immediate action, digital game purchases will become mere temporary rentals, fundamentally undermining consumer rights and digital ownership.
